title: practical english usage - Part 214
content:
I found more blackberries than you. (= ... than you found.) The weather isn't as good as last year. (= ... as it was last year.) We   can leave out words after as and than, if the meaning is clear.

7 comparative         structures        with    as  and   than ‘ Have a good time.' 'I'll try.' (= I’ ll try to have a good time.) Come when you want. (= ... when you want to come.) Sometimes a whole infinitive, including to, is left out.

I don't dance much  now, but I used to a lot.

'Areyou and Gillian getting married? *We hope to.’ (= We   hope We   can use to instead of repeating a whole infinitive. For details, ►    280.

He says he hasn't any friends, but I know he has.

I thought she would be angry, and she was.

The  same structures are possible with non-auxiliary be and have.

I was planning to go to Paris next week, but I can't. (= . .. 1 can't go to Paris This type of ellipsis can include complements that follow the verb.

She said she'd phone, but she didn't. (= ... didn’ t phone.) 7 haven't paid.1 7 haven't either.' (= ... I haven't paid either.) Auxiliary verbs are often used alone instead of full verbs. For details, ►    279.

5  at  the   end   of  a  verb    phrase Information StructureSection 25 grammar •  276  ellipsis with and, but and or I have always paid my bills and I always will (pay...).

We   can sometimes drop a verb that is repeated in a different form.

Jake (washed the dishes) and Sophie washed the dishes.

The food (is ready) and the drinks are ready.

She was poor but (she was) happy.

walked across America.

We drove (across America), rode (across America), flew (across America) and The Minister likes golf but (the Minister) hates fishing.

ripe apples and (ripe) pears She can read, but (she) can't write.

these men and (these) women in France, (in) Germany or (in) Spain a knife and (a) fork       antique (furniture) or modern furniture words or phrases of various kinds.

When  expressions are joined by and, but or or, we  often leave out repeated 1 various      kinds    of  word    left   out 276 ellipsis     with    and,   but   and   or DOG  FINDS ROMAN  TREASURE Single man looking for flat Oxford area.

Take 500g butter and place in small saucepan.

For details, ►    291.

In certain styles, many or all non-essential words can be dropped.

14 abbreviated         styles I'd like a piece of toast with butter on (it).

My socks have got holes in (them).

descriptive structures.

prepositions. This happens, for example, when have or with are used in In British English, pronoun objects can sometimes be dropped after 13  pronouns       after    prepositions For cases like We need a place to live (in), ►    554.

What time shall I come? (More natural than At what time...?) We're staying here (for) another three months.

See you (on) Monday night.

(►  214.2-214.6).

In an informal style, prepositions can be dropped in a few time expressions 12 prepositions Leave in oven until browned on top.

I'm enclosing the signed contract, as agreed.

Phone me if (it is) necessary.  He had a small heart attack while asleep.

When ordering please send £1.50  for postage and packing.

(= Though  he was intelligent.. .) Though  intelligent, he was very poorly educated.

grammar •  277  ellipsis at the beginning of a sentence Careful what you say. (= Be careful...) Nobody at home. (= There’ s nobody at home.) Keeping well, I hope? (= You're keeping well...) Seen Joe? (= Have you seen Joe?) Won't work, you know. (= It won’ t work .. .) Must dash. (= I must dash.) Couldn't understand a word. (= I couldn’ t...) Wife's away. (= My   wife’ s ...) Car's running badly. (= The  car’ s ...) (I, you, etc), auxiliary verbs (am, have, etc) and the preparatory subject there.

include articles (the, a/an), possessives (my, your, etc), personal pronouns of a sentence if the meaning is clear without them. Words that can be left out In informal spoken English we  often leave out unstressed words at the beginning 1  words     that    can   be   left   out 277 ellipsis     at  the   beginning       of  a  sentence For singular and plural verbs with neither ... nor, ►    228.
